---
author: "James Hetherington"
date: 2026-07-22
format: revealjs
title: "A talk about Automating discovery"
---

# Let's learn a game

# Why isn't chess like science?

:::: {.two-col}
::: {}
* You know where all the pieces are.
* You know what the valid moves are.
* You know what counts as a win or a draw.
:::
::: {}
{{< image-credit width="60%" src="assets/generated/starting_position.svg" alt="The starting position of a chess game" >}}
:::
::::

# How to play Kriegspiel

:::: {.two-col}
::: {}
For Kriegspiel, you need three boards in three rooms

The referee has the only board with the real positions of all the pieces.
:::
::: {}
{{< image-credit width="100%" src="assets/stored/cartoon.png" alt="Three rooms with chess boards" >}}
:::
::::

# How to play Kriegspiel

:::: {.two-col}
::: {}
Suppose the position is this:
{{< image-credit width="40%" src="assets/generated/kriegspiel_position_1.svg" alt="A Kriegspiel position" >}}

...I play Qd8. 
:::
::: {}
The referee tells me "illegal move, there is a pawn on d5".

{{< image-credit width="60%" src="assets/generated/kriegspiel_position_2.svg" alt="A Kriegspiel position" >}}
:::
::::

# What does Kriegspiel tell us about science?

:::: {.two-col}
::: {}
* We can play to try to achieve some engineering objective
* But we also play to learn about the world
:::
::: {}
* We evaluate a move by how much it reduces uncertainty, as well as whether it achieves the objective.
* We call this a "partially observed decision problem" (PODP).
:::
::::

# Simulating gravity

Simulations and models help us write down our current view of how the world works.

We can make simulators of gravity:

{{< image-credit width="60%" src="https://research.jh.arc.ucl.ac.uk/inaugural/assets/generated/nice.mp4" alt="A simulation of gravity" >}}

# Physics-free gravity

* We can **observe** lots of pictures of planets.
* Try to **learn** the rules of gravity from those observations.

:::: {.two-col}
::: {}
{{< image-credit
  src="https://jamespjh.github.io/inaugural-lecture/assets/generated/scatter_grid_5x5.png"
  alt="25 images of the sky" 
  width="60%"
>}}
:::
::: {}
{{< video-credit
  src="https://research.jh.arc.ucl.ac.uk/inaugural/assets/generated/power_law_convergence.mp4"
  alt="Animation showing the power-law model's trajectory converging toward the true inverse-square law trajectory as training progresses through each optimisation step"
  width="100%" >}}
:::
::::

# The triumph of stamp collecting.

{{< image-credit
src="https://research.jh.arc.ucl.ac.uk/inaugural/assets/stored/stamp.jpg"
alt="A magnifying glass inspecting a stamp collection" 
credit="Wikimedia commons, Heptagon, CC-SA"
width="40%"
credit-url="https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Magnifying_glass2.jpg"
>}}

"All science is either physics or stamp collecting" - attributed to Ernest Rutherford, perhaps apocryphal.

# Uncertainty in physics-free gravity

{{< video-credit
  src="https://jamespjh.github.io/inaugural-lecture/assets/generated/probabilistic_convergence.mp4"
  alt="Animation showing the probabilistic power-law model's trajectory converging toward the true inverse-square law trajectory as training progresses through each optimisation step"
  width="45%" >}}

# What if we could move the planets?

:::: {.two-col}
::: {}
{{< video-credit
  src="https://jamespjh.github.io/inaugural-lecture/assets/generated/probabilistic_convergence.mp4"
  alt="Animation showing the probabilistic power-law model's trajectory converging toward the true inverse-square law trajectory as training progresses through each optimisation step"
  width="75%" >}}
:::
::: {}
{{< video-credit
src="https://jamespjh.github.io/inaugural-lecture/assets/generated/acquisition_convergence.mp4"
alt="Animation showing the acquisition function model's trajectory converging toward the true inverse-square law trajectory as training progresses through each optimisation step"
width="90%" >}}
:::
::::

# Automated experimentation

{{< image-credit
src="assets/generated/triangle.svg"
alt="The future of automated experimentation"
width="80%" >}}
